elements.sent-2411-001: In recent decades, chemistry publications and patents have increased rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-002: A significant portion of key information is embedded in molecular structure figures, complicating large-scale literature searches and limiting the application of large language models in fields such as biology, chemistry, and pharmaceuticals.
elements.sent-2411-003: The automatic extraction of precise chemical structures is of critical importance.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the presence of numerous Markush structures in real-world documents, along with variations in molecular image quality, drawing styles, and noise, significantly limits the performance of existing optical chemical structure recognition (OCSR) methods.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present MolParser, a novel end-to-end OCSR method that efficiently and accurately recognizes chemical structures from real-world documents, including difficult Markush structure.
elements.sent-2411-006: We use a extended SMILES encoding rule to annotate our training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-007: Under this rule, we build MolParser-7M, the largest annotated molecular image dataset to our knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-008: While utilizing a large amount of synthetic data, we employed active learning methods to incorporate substantial in-the-wild data, specifically samples cropped from real patents and scientific literature, into the training process.
elements.sent-2411-009: We trained an end-to-end molecular image captioning model, MolParser, using a curriculum learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-010: MolParser significantly outperforms classical and learning-based methods across most scenarios, with potential for broader downstream applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: The dataset is publicly available in \href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/AI4Industry/MolParser-7M {huggingface .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/overall.png} \caption{MolParser uses an end-to-end transformer to extract the chemical structures to string expression from the real patent or literature. We extend the SMILES format to enable the representation of more complex molecular structures including Markush.} \label{fig:overall}
elements.sent-2411-012: A significant portion of chemical information remains locked in unstructured formats within printed or digital documents, such as patents and scientific papers.
elements.sent-2411-013: In many of these documents, especially in the field of chemistry, molecular structures are often depicted as images.
elements.sent-2411-014: These chemical structures figures are critically important for drug discovery, patent examination, and the retrieval of chemical data.
elements.sent-2411-015: But they are difficult to extract into machine-readable text.
elements.sent-2411-016: Automatically parsing molecular structures from these document images, a process known as Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR), becomes particularly important.
elements.sent-2411-017: With the growing popularity of Large Language Models (LLMs), many efforts are now focusing on applying them to the understanding scientific literature.
elements.sent-2411-018: Parsing molecular structure images into structured, understandable text also unlocks the potential of large models in processing patents and literature in chemistry-related fields.
elements.sent-2411-019: OCSR aims to automatically convert chemical structure diagrams from scientific literature, patents, and other scanned documents into machine-readable string such as SMILES ~\cite{weininger1988smiles representation.}
elements.sent-2411-020: SMILES, though widely used for molecular representation, has notable limitations in handling complex chemical entities.
elements.sent-2411-021: It struggles with representing Markush structures, which are used in patents to describe a broad class of molecules by allowing variability at certain positions, enabling the protection of entire families of compounds.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, SMILES cannot effectively handle connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments, duplicated groups or polymers, all of which require a level of flexibility that its linear format does not support.
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, SMILES is not well-suited for tasks involving large models, such as Markush-molecule matching, as its structure lacks the clarity and hierarchical organization needed for efficient interpretation by machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-024: These limitations hinder its utility in advanced cheminformatics applications.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/noise.png} \caption{In-the-wild problem in OCSR. In real world patent and literature, we utilize object detection to locate and extract molecular images. However, there are several challenging cases that need to be addressed. These include (a) abbreviations and Markush structures, (b) image noise, blur and interference from surrounding elements, and (c) various drawing styles.} \label{fig:noise}
elements.sent-2411-025: The complexity of OCSR arises from the intricate nature of chemical diagrams, which not only include atoms and bonds but also various annotations, nested structures, and ring connections.
elements.sent-2411-026: These elements make traditional Optical Character Recognition (OCR) techniques inadequate for this task.
elements.sent-2411-027: Another challenge of the OCSR task lies in the varying styles and visual representations of chemical diagrams, which can differ in terms of drawing styles, colors, and formatting.
elements.sent-2411-028: These variations complicate the extraction of molecular structures.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, during the recognition process, non-chemical elements such as surrounding text or other images from the paper may be mistakenly captured, further hindering the accurate identification of the chemical structures.
elements.sent-2411-030: These issues, combined with the noise, distortions, and font variations often found in document images, make the task even more complex.
elements.sent-2411-031: Early OCSR approaches follow a graph reconstruction paradigm, where molecular structures were rebuilt by identifying key components such as atoms, bonds, and charges.
elements.sent-2411-032: These were typically extracted using hand-crafted image processing techniques, with rules applied to connect the elements and form a graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-033: While some recent methods have introduced deep learning for atom and bond detection, they still rely on hand-crafted rules ~\cite{filippov2009optical, smolov2011imago, oldenhof2020chemgrapher, xu2022molminer, zhang2022abc for linking the recognized components.}
elements.sent-2411-034: There are also some new methods that utilize Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) or transformers as a replacement for traditional rule-based approaches ~\cite{morin2023molgrapher, chen2024molnextr .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Construct molecular structures uses graph representations and ultimately derives the string representation of these molecules (e.g. SMILES).
elements.sent-2411-036: This multi-step process makes training and model fine-tuning relatively complex and limits the robustness of these methods when dealing with noise and distortions commonly found in real-world documents and patents.
elements.sent-2411-037: As a result, their performance in handling noisy data remains suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-038: With the recent advancements in deep learning, end-to-end neural network models have become the dominant approach for OCSR.
elements.sent-2411-039: These methods combine image recognition with sequence generation tasks, directly converting input images into string molecular representations ~\cite{clevert2021img2mol, xu2022swinocsr, rajan2023decimer .}
elements.sent-2411-040: This method can be seen as a special case of image caption.
elements.sent-2411-041: However, despite significant progress, current models face real-world challenges, particularly when processing complex, noisy, or previously unseen chemical structures in patent documents.
elements.sent-2411-042: An important reason for this is that the training data utilized by these methods is often small-scale synthetic data, which differs significantly from real-world data scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-043: This highlights the need for developing more robust and versatile OCSR systems capable of handling greater diversity and complexity in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-044: In order to better address the challenges of the OCSR task in real-world literature.
elements.sent-2411-045: We introduce a new end-to-end framework named MolParser for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition in the wild, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:overall The main contributions of this paper are: Extened SMILES We extend the SMILES representation to accommodate a broader range of specialized molecules commonly found in patents and literature, including Markush structures, connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments, duplicated groups, and polymer structures.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, this extended SMILES format is compatible with RDKit ~\cite{rdkit and is also LLM-friendly, making it convenient for using LLMs to perform various analyses and processing on molecules..}
elements.sent-2411-047: MolParser-7M Datasets Based on our extended SMILES representation, we construct MolParser-7M , the largest annotated molecular recognition training dataset to our knowledge, with over 7 million paired image-SMILES data.
elements.sent-2411-048: MolParser-7M contains a large amount of diverse synthetic data, as well as in-the-wild data (cropped from real-world PDF scans).
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, we design a human-in-the-loop data engine to extract the most training-relevant molecular images from millions of patents and scientific papers, followed by meticulous manual annotation and cross review.
elements.sent-2411-050: We also provide a new OCSR benchmark, WildMol-20k, including 10k ordinary molecules and 10k Markush structures.
elements.sent-2411-051: All the samples are annotated by our extended SMILES format.
elements.sent-2411-052: MolParser Model We regard OCSR tasks as a special type of image captioning task, where the content of the caption is an extended SMILES string.
elements.sent-2411-053: We develop MolParser model using an end-to-end image caption architecture, which includes a vision encoder, a feature compressor, and a BART \cite{lewis2019bart decoder to generate extended SMILES strings.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We employed curriculum learning to train the MolParser model, first pretraining it on the diverse synthetic data of the MolParser7M dataset, gradually increasing the intensity of data augmentation during training.
elements.sent-2411-055: Afterward, we fine-tuned the model on a subset containing 300k in-the-wild real data.
elements.sent-2411-056: As a result, in USPTO ~\cite{filippov2009optical benchmark, MolParser achieved state-of-the-art accuracy exceeding 98\%.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In WildMol-20k, we also attain over 91\% Tanimoto similarity score, surpassing the current state-of-the-art method, MolGrapher, which has a score of 60\%".

elements.sent-2411-079: We extend the SMILES representation method, to more effectively represent the Markush structures commonly found in patents, as well as complex compositions such as connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments, duplicated groups, and polymer structures.
elements.sent-2411-080: Additionally, we ensure that this approach is compatible with RDKit and LLM-friendly, facilitating subsequent analysis and processing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-081: The extended SMILES format will be denoted as: SMILES<sep>EXTENSION.
elements.sent-2411-082: Where SMILES refers to the RDKit-compatible SMILES representation.
elements.sent-2411-083: Special token <sep> serves as the separator, and optional EXTENSION represents supplementary descriptive used to handle complex cases such as Markush structures.. In EXTENSION.
elements.sent-2411-084: We use some XML-like special tokens to represent certain special functional groups.
elements.sent-2411-085: For Markush R-groups and abbreviation groups, we add special token and to encapsulate descriptions of these special substituents.
elements.sent-2411-086: Similarly, we use special token <r> and </r> for ring attachments; <c> and </c> for abstract rings.
elements.sent-2411-087: Additionally, there is a special token <dum> representing a connection point.
elements.sent-2411-088: For the specific description of each functional group, we use the following format: [INDEX]:[GROUP\_NAME].
elements.sent-2411-089: Figure \ref{fig:smiles shows a example of our extended SMILES.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Although there are numerous complex Markush structures in actual patents, our extended SMILES rules can still adequately address these cases.

elements.sent-2411-092: Motivated by image captioning approach ~\cite{li2023trocr, xu2022swinocsr, lee2023pix2struct, blecher2023nougat , we employ a single transformer only architecture to translate molecular structure images into our extended SMILES format.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Our MolParser model has three components, an image encoder, a feature compresser, and a SMILES decoder.
elements.sent-2411-094: We use an ImageNet \cite{deng2009imagenet pretrained Swin Transformer \cite{liu2021swin as the image encoder for our MolParser.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Similar to LLaVA \cite{liu2024llava , we use a two-layer MLP as a vision-language connector and as a feature compressor to reduce the number of input channels.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Finally, we use a decoder-only transformer architecture, BART \cite{lewis2019bart , to decode the compressed image features and predict the SMILES sequence (our extended SMILES format) through next-token prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-097: We employ next-token prediction to generate the extended SMILES sequence.
elements.sent-2411-098: For paired training data, we first use a tokenizer to convert the extended SMILES into a token sequence.
elements.sent-2411-099: Each token generally represents an atom, an abbreviated group name, a number, a special symbol, or a special token defined in extended SMILES.
elements.sent-2411-100: The training process is conducted in two stages: pretraining stage and supervised finetuning stage.
elements.sent-2411-101: In the first stage, we conduct pretraining using a synthetic dataset comprising over 7.7 million paired training samples.
elements.sent-2411-102: At the same time, we use curriculum learning method in pretraining stage to get better convergence.
elements.sent-2411-103: Concurrently, we employ a curriculum learning \cite{bengio2009curriculum approach during pretraining to achieve better convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-104: In the early phase of pretraining, we refrain from utilizing data augmentation and restrict our focus to simple molecules with a SMILES token count of fewer than 60.
elements.sent-2411-105: Subsequently, we progressively increase the intensity of data augmentation and incorporate molecules with longer token sequences into the training process.
elements.sent-2411-106: The detail of training dataset is described in Section \ref{sec:datasets In the second stage, we fine-tune the model with a set of human-annotated in-the-wild data.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The purpose of this stage is to further enhance the generalization ability of MolParser in the real scene.
elements.sent-2411-108: Previous methods \cite{clevert2021img2mol, morin2023molgrapher, rajan2023decimer only use synthetic data, which exhibited a relatively restricted distribution in images and consisted of relatively simple molecular structures, resulting in suboptimal performance in real-world applications.}
elements.sent-2411-109: In contrast, we employ an active learning approach to construct a data engine that extracted approximately 400,000 molecular images deemed most valuable for model learning from over 1.22 million real patents and academic papers, supplemented by manual annotation and secondary review.
elements.sent-2411-110: After incorporating these data for fine-tuning, we further improve MolParser’s ability in real application.

elements.sent-2411-112: As paired OCSR training data is not abundant on the Internet, we build a data engine to enable the collection of our 7.7 million OCSR paired dataset, MolParser-7M, with low cost.
elements.sent-2411-113: The data engine has two stages: (1) start up phase with fully synthetic training data, (2) active learning with human in the loop.
elements.sent-2411-114: Here is the detail: Start up with synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-115: Due to the significant difficulty in annotating molecular data in SMILES format, the cost of manual annotation in large volumes is quite high.
elements.sent-2411-116: Our analysis shows that, on average, it takes over three minutes for an expert to annotate a single molecule from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-117: Therefore, we chose to initiate the data engine with synthetic data and gradually expand it to real data.
elements.sent-2411-118: We utilized a diverse range of molecular structure sources and various image rendering methods, generating over 7 million pairs of images and extended-SMILES.

elements.sent-2411-121: Active Learning with Human in the Loop.
elements.sent-2411-122: To further enhance the generalization performance of our model, we extract molecular training data directly from patents and scientific literature in PDF format and manually annotate the molecular structures.
elements.sent-2411-123: Initially, we train a YOLO11 ~\cite{yolo11 object detection model to locate molecules within these documents.}
elements.sent-2411-124: We use 1.22 million real PDF files, including patent documents from various international patent offices and open-access papers from Internet such as bioRxiv, medRxiv, and ChemRxiv.
elements.sent-2411-125: From this dataset, we extract over 20 million molecular images.
elements.sent-2411-126: After employing image p-hash similarity analysis to remove duplicate or highly similar molecular images, this number reduces to 4 million.
elements.sent-2411-127: Due to the sheer volume of this dataset, manual annotation of all images is not feasible.
elements.sent-2411-128: Therefore, we introduce active learning algorithms to identify and select samples with higher importance.
elements.sent-2411-129: We perform 5-fold training using generated data, resulting in five distinct models, and conduct inference with each model on the data.
elements.sent-2411-130: Consequently, for each molecule, five extended SMILES strings are predicted.
elements.sent-2411-131: We extract the standard SMILES sequences before the <sep> token and compute the pairwise Tanimoto similarity, taking the average as the confidence score for the molecule's prediction.
elements.sent-2411-132: We observe that data with low confidence scores often correspond to images of poor quality, whereas data with high confidence scores typically indicate a high probability of correct predictions.
elements.sent-2411-133: We then randomly select samples with confidence scores between 0.6 and 0.9, as we believe these samples present significant recognition challenges and are highly beneficial for training.
elements.sent-2411-134: We manually annotate the molecular structures for these selected samples.
elements.sent-2411-135: During the annotation process, we use the model's predictions as pre-annotations, which annotators then modify as needed.
elements.sent-2411-136: The annotated results are subsequently reviewed by a second annotator for double-check.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our analysis shows that by leveraging pre-annotations, the annotation time per molecule is reduced to just 30 seconds, resulting in an approximately 90\% reduction in manual annotation labor costs compared to annotating from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-138: After every 80,000 completed annotations, we incorporate these data into the training set, update the 5-fold models, and repeat the active learning process.
elements.sent-2411-139: This iterative approach allows the model's generalization ability and the quality of the pre-annotated data to improve in tandem.
elements.sent-2411-140: Ultimately, through this active learning loop, we compile a dataset of 400,000 manually annotated images.

elements.sent-2411-215: The extended SMILES format is defined as: \begin{center SMILES<sep>EXTENSION \end{center.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: SMILES represents an RDKit-compatible SMILES expression.
elements.sent-2411-217: Each molecule has a unique representation that can be generated (for non-Markush molecules) using the following method, where rootedAtAtom=0 indicates that the SMILES generation starts from the atom indexed at 0.
elements.sent-2411-218: The part before the delimiter is the RDKit-compatible SMILES, while the part after provides supplemental information (e.g., Markush groups, connection points, repeating groups).
elements.sent-2411-219: EXTENSION is an optional component that supplements the preceding SMILES with descriptions written in XML format, including groups surrounded by special tokens of three types: \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-220: An additional special token <dum> indicates a connection point.. Definitions:.
elements.sent-2411-221: ATOM\_INDEX refers to the atom index at which the substituent is located (starting from 0).
elements.sent-2411-222: RING\_INDEX denotes the ring index (starting from 0).
elements.sent-2411-223: GROUP\_NAME specifies the name of the substituent, which can be an abbreviated group, general substituent, or Markush group, such as R, X, Y, Z, Ph, Me, OMe, CF3, etc. It may also be <dum> to indicate a connection point.
elements.sent-2411-224: For Markush substituents with superscripts or subscripts, these can be represented within square brackets, e.g., R[1], R[3].
elements.sent-2411-225: CIRCLE\_INDEX refers to the index of the named ring (starting from 0).
elements.sent-2411-226: CIRCLE\_NAME indicates the name of the ring.

elements.sent-2411-330: We use an extended SMILES encoding rule to annotate our training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-331: Under this rule, we build MolParser-7M, a large-scale OCSR dataset based on our E-SMILES representation.
elements.sent-2411-332: In many of these documents, particularly in the field of chemistry, molecular structures are presented as images.
elements.sent-2411-333: These graphical depictions are essential for drug discovery, patent analysis, and chemical information retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-334: However, extracting them into machine-readable text remains a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-335: The automated interpretation of molecular structures from document images, a task known as Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR), is therefore of growing importance.
elements.sent-2411-336: With the rise of large language models (LLMs), increasing efforts are being directed toward applying them to the understanding of scientific literature.
elements.sent-2411-337: Converting molecular structure images into structured, interpretable text not only advances OCSR but also enables LLMs to more effectively process patents and scientific documents in chemistry-related domains.
elements.sent-2411-338: Additionally, SMILES cannot effectively handle connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments with uncertainty position, duplicated structures or polymers, all of which require a level of flexibility that its linear format does not support.
elements.sent-2411-339: There are also some new methods that utilize Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) or transformers as replacements for traditional rule-based approaches~\cite{morin2023molgrapher, chen2024molnextr .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Constructing molecular structures uses graph representations and ultimately derives the string representation of these molecules (e.g. SMILES).
elements.sent-2411-341: We introduce a new end-to-end framework named MolParser for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition in the wild, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:overall The main contributions of this paper are: Extended SMILES We extend the SMILES representation to accommodate a broader range of specialized molecules commonly found in patents and literature, including Markush structures, connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments with uncertainty position, duplicated structures, and polymer structures.}
elements.sent-2411-342: Additionally, this extended SMILES format is compatible with RDKit ~\cite{rdkit and is also LLM-friendly, making it convenient for using LLMs to perform various analyses and processing on molecules.}
elements.sent-2411-343: Related work includes various molecular representation methods, such as SMILES, as well as algorithms for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR).. SMILES variants.
elements.sent-2411-344: However, this approach has limited scalability, as it struggles to support abstract rings, ring attachments with uncertainty position, duplicated structures, and polymer structures.
elements.sent-2411-345: Similar methods include MMSSC-Net \cite{zhang2024mmssc and MolScribe \cite{qian2023molscribe .. Markush Recognition.}}
elements.sent-2411-346: Markush structures are chemical representations that use variable groups to describe a family of related compounds.
elements.sent-2411-347: They are commonly found in patents to claim broad molecular classes.
elements.sent-2411-348: These structures often contain R-groups, repeating units, and variable attachments.
elements.sent-2411-349: Markush recognition \cite{haupt2009markush, beard2020chemschematicresolver, wang2022multi, morin2025markushgrapher remains a major challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-350: Moreover, existing tools such as RDKit \cite{rdkit cannot parse Markush structures.}
elements.sent-2411-351: They do not support inputs with undefined groups or variable atoms.
elements.sent-2411-352: These tools also fail to render valid images of Markush structures.
elements.sent-2411-353: As a result, generating large-scale training data becomes more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-354: The lack of standardized representation and visualization limits data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-355: This creates a bottleneck for training robust models on Markush recognition.
elements.sent-2411-356: We extend the SMILES representation method, abbreviated as E-SMILES, to more effectively represent the Markush structures commonly found in patents, as well as complex compositions such as connection points, abstract rings, ring attachments with uncertainty position, duplicated structures, and polymer structures.
elements.sent-2411-357: For Markush R-groups and abbreviation groups, we add special tokens and to encapsulate descriptions of these special substituents.
elements.sent-2411-358: Similarly, we use special tokens <r> and </r> for ring attachments with uncertainty position; <c> and </c> for abstract rings.
elements.sent-2411-359: Finally, we adopt a decoder-only Transformer architecture, BART-Decoder \cite{lewis2019bart , to decode the compressed image features and autoregressively generate the extended SMILES (E-SMILES) sequence via next-token prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-360: We employ autoregressive algorithm to generate the E-SMILES sequence.
elements.sent-2411-361: For paired training data, we first use a tokenizer to convert the E-SMILES sequence into a token sequence.
elements.sent-2411-362: Each token generally represents an atom, an abbreviated group name, a number, a special symbol, or a special token defined in E-SMILES.
elements.sent-2411-363: The detail of training dataset is described in Section \ref{sec:datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-364: In the second stage, we fine-tune the model with a set of human-annotated in-the-wild data.
elements.sent-2411-365: The detail of our active leanring data engine is described in Section \ref{sec:engine .}
elements.sent-2411-366: Initially, we train a YOLO11 ~\cite{yolo11 object detection model, named as MolDet, to locate molecules within these documents.}
elements.sent-2411-367: We perform 5-fold training on synthetic data to obtain five distinct models.
elements.sent-2411-368: Each model independently generates predictions, resulting in five E-SMILES strings per molecule image.
elements.sent-2411-369: We extract the standard SMILES sequences before the <sep> token and compute the pairwise Tanimoto similarity, taking the average similarity score as the confidence score for OCSR prediction.
elements.sent-2411-370: We observe that data with low confidence scores often correspond to images with poor quality, whereas data with high confidence scores typically indicate a high probability of correct predictions.
elements.sent-2411-371: During the annotation process, we use model predictions as pre-annotations, which annotators modify as needed, without editing molecular structures from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-372: Two different annotators independently review the model predictions or manual modifications to verify their correctness.
elements.sent-2411-373: Analysis shows that leveraging pre-annotations reduces annotation time per molecule to 30 seconds, achieving approximately 90\% savings in manual labor compared to annotation from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-374: After every 80,000 completed annotations, we incorporate the data into the training set, update the 5-fold models, and repeat the active learning cycle.
elements.sent-2411-375: This iterative process enhances both the model's generalization ability and the quality of the pre-annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-376: Through this loop, we construct a dataset of 400,000 manually annotated images.
elements.sent-2411-377: In the final dataset, 56.04\% of annotations directly use model pre-annotations, 20.97\% pass review after a single manual correction, 13.87\% are accepted after a second round of annotation, and 9.13\% require three or more rounds of annotation.

elements.sent-2411-383: We make the unexpected observation that the image feature extractor of our MolParser, a Swin Transformer, can serve as an effective molecular fingerprint (or molecular embedding) for downstream molecular property prediction tasks after being trained on the MolParser-7M dataset.
elements.sent-2411-384: Specifically, for each molecule, we first use the RDKit toolkit \cite{rdkit to render a 2D molecule structural image, then extract visual features using the MolParser vision backbone, and apply global average pooling to obtain a 2048-dimensional feature vector as the molecular representation.}
elements.sent-2411-385: A simple two-layer MLP is then used to perform molecular property prediction.
elements.sent-2411-386: We evaluate our approach alongside several baselines on five molecular property classification tasks from the MoleculeNet benchmark \cite{wu2018moleculenet .}
elements.sent-2411-387: As shown in Table \ref{tab:molnet , our method achieves competitive performance in molecular property prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-388: Notably, features extracted by MolParser, when paired with a lightweight MLP, perform on par with more complex models that rely on 2D or 3D graph-based representations.
elements.sent-2411-389: Moreover, our approach substantially outperforms other image-based feature extractors that are not pre-trained on the MolParser-7M dataset.
elements.sent-2411-390: These results indicate that 2D molecular structure images contain rich, chemically meaningful information.
elements.sent-2411-391: They also highlight the effectiveness of our end-to-end, large-scale OCSR training in learning high-quality visual representations for downstream chemical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-392: Following OmniParser \cite{lu2024omniparser , we input the molecular locations detected by our MolDet model and the E-SMILES sequences recognized by our MolParser model into GPT-4o \cite{gpt4o to enhance chemical reaction parsing.}}
elements.sent-2411-393: We draw bounding boxes around target molecules in chemical reaction images and label each with a corresponding molecule index, which is provided as input to GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-394: Additionally, we prepend the prompt with the E-SMILES recognized for each indexed molecule.
elements.sent-2411-395: This significantly enhances the ability of MLLMs like GPT-4o to process chemical reaction images.
elements.sent-2411-396: Refer to the appendix for details.
elements.sent-2411-397: We propose a novel end-to-end OCSR algorithm that extends the SMILES representation and introduces a large-scale training dataset, MolParser-7M.
elements.sent-2411-398: The model is pretrained on large scale synthesized data and fine-tuned on manually annotated in-the-wild samples.
elements.sent-2411-399: It outperforms existing methods on classical OCSR benchmarks and our newly introduced WildMol benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-400: The system achieves high processing speed and strong performance in extracting structured molecular information from real, unstructured scientific literature.
elements.sent-2411-401: Despite its effectiveness, MolParser still has room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-402: For instance, molecular chirality, which is closely related to chemical properties, is not yet fully exploited.
elements.sent-2411-403: In addition, scaling up the amount of real annotated training data may further boost performance.
elements.sent-2411-404: As future work, we aim to address the challenge of chirality in OCSR and scale up the volume of real-world training data.
elements.sent-2411-405: We also plan to use MolParser to extract molecules and Markush structures with their visual fingerprints from large-scale scientific literature and patents, enabling the creation of a comprehensive database for chemical information mining.
elements.sent-2411-406: MolParser-7M dataset is open sourced in \href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/AI4Industry/MolParser-7M {HuggingFace Dataset .}}
elements.sent-2411-407: The yolo11 model used for detection molecule structure is also avaliable in \href{https://huggingface.co/AI4Industry/MolDet {HuggingFace Model .}}
elements.sent-2411-408: We also provide a \href{https://ocsr.dp.tech/ {OCSR demo using our MolParser-Base model.}}

elements.sent-2411-409: SMILES Augmentation We apply SMILES augmentation only during pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-410: Since a molecule's SMILES representation varies with the choice of root atom, we randomly change the root atom to help the transformer learn SMILES syntax more robustly.
elements.sent-2411-411: During fine-tuning, augmentation is disabled and the root atom is fixed to index zero, reducing ambiguity during generation.
elements.sent-2411-412: All variants of MolParser adopt a BART decoder with 12 transformer decoder layers and 16 attention heads.
elements.sent-2411-413: An MLP connector reduces the channel dimension of the visual encoder output by half.
elements.sent-2411-414: The Swin Transformer produces a feature map of size bs x n × n × d, which is flattened into a sequence and used as prefix tokens for the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-415: During the pre-training stage, we train the model for 20 epochs using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4, a weight decay of 1e-2, and a cosine learning rate schedule with warmup.
elements.sent-2411-417: In the fine-tuning phase, we reduce the learning rate to 5e-5, decrease the number of training epochs to 4, and lower the label smoothing to 0.005.
elements.sent-2411-418: All experiments are conducted on 8 NVIDIA RTX 4090D GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-419: In unstructured documents, extracting molecular structures and leveraging LLMs for structured information extraction has become a key application of Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR).
elements.sent-2411-420: We first convert each PDF page into an image and use a YOLO11 \cite{yolo11 model to detect molecular structures.}
elements.sent-2411-421: The detected molecules are then parsed by our MolParser and converted into an extended, XML-like SMILES format that is more LLM-friendly.
elements.sent-2411-422: This representation allows LLMs to easily identify which groups undergo transformations in chemical reactions.
elements.sent-2411-423: Following OmniParser \cite{lu2024omniparser , we integrate molecular location and SMILES information into GPT-4o \cite{gpt4o to enhance MolParser’s ability to parse full chemical reaction formulas.}}
